verbatim: [§22] Of the men therefore who have accompanied us all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and went out among us, [§23] beginning from the baptism of John, to the day that he was received up from us, of these one must become a witness with us of his resurrection.
extracted_rules.reasoning: When selecting a replacement for Judas, Peter explicitly limits the pool of candidates to 'the men' (andrown) who had been with them, thereby excluding the women mentioned in paragraph 15 from being considered for the official office of witness/apostle.

verbatim: [§45] 'It will be in the last days, says God, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh. Your sons and your daughters will prophesy. Your young men will see visions.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a prophetic rule where divine inspiration (visions) is specifically granted to the young as a distinct group within the community.

verbatim: [§45] 'It will be in the last days, says God, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh... Your old men will dream dreams.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a specific form of spiritual resource (divine dreams) associated with the age group of 'old men'.

verbatim: [§46] Yes, and on my servants and on my handmaidens in those days, I will pour out my Spirit, and they will prophesy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author quotes Joel to establish that even those in a state of servitude (servants/handmaidens) are granted the resource of prophecy through the Holy Spirit.

verbatim: [§28] All who believed were together, and had all things common. They sold their possessions and goods, and distributed them to all, according as anyone had need.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The nascent Christian community operated under a norm where individual owners of goods and possessions surrendered their exclusive rights to those materials for the benefit of the group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§72] All who believed were together, and had all things common. [§73] They sold their possessions and goods, and distributed them to all, according as anyone had need.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic social organization among the group of believers where individual ownership is replaced by communal distribution based on need.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to individual earnings from sales
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§103] ...for as many as were owners of lands or houses sold them, and brought the prices of the things that were sold, and laid them at the apostles' feet, and distribution was made to each, according as anyone had need.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule establishes that members of this religious group surrendered the proceeds of capital sales to central leadership (apostles) for redistribution.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§103] They called them, and charged them not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The ruling council (Sanhedrin) imposed a formal legal restriction on the apostles' ability to communicate their religious message.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§103] Annas the high priest was there, with Caiaphas, John, Alexander, and as many as were relatives of the high priest. When they had stood them in the midst, they inquired...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies membership in the high priest's family as a criterion for inclusion in the governing body/council that held legal inquiries.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§99] It will be, that every soul that will not listen to that prophet will be utterly destroyed from among the people.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a religious law (attributed to Moses) stating that individuals who do not follow the designated religious authority lose the right to exist within the community.

verbatim: [§135] The multitude of those who believed were of one heart and soul. Not one of them claimed that anything of the things which he possessed was his own, but they had all things common.

[§137] For neither was there among them any who lacked, for as many as were owners of lands or houses sold them, and brought the prices of the things that were sold, [§138] and laid them at the apostles' feet, and distribution was made to each, according as anyone had need.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic social arrangement among the early Christian community where private ownership was voluntarily surrendered in favor of a redistribution system managed by the apostles.

verbatim: [§145] While you kept it, didn't it remain your own? After it was sold, wasn't it in your power? How is it that you have conceived this thing in your heart? You haven't lied to men, but to God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the Ananias episode, Peter explicitly acknowledges the legal right of the individual to retain their property and the proceeds of its sale, clarifying that the communal sharing was not a compulsory legal seizure but a voluntary practice.

verbatim: [§120] But so that this spreads no further among the people, let's threaten them, that from now on they don't speak to anyone in this name."
[§121] They called them, and charged them not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Jewish religious council (Sanhedrin) issues a formal prohibition against the apostles teaching or speaking about Jesus, which constitutes a restriction on the specific group's freedom of expression.

verbatim: [§179] Now I tell you, refrain from these men, and leave them alone. For if this counsel or this work is of men, it will be overthrown. [§180] But if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow it, and you would be found even to be fighting against God!" [§181] They agreed with him. Summoning the apostles, they beat them and charged them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and let them go.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following Gamaliel's legal advice, the council shifts from a determination to kill the apostles to a lesser punishment (beating and release), establishing a precedent of restraint in legal proceedings against this group.

verbatim: [§187] Therefore select from among you, brothers, seven men of good report, full of the Holy Spirit and of wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In response to a communal need for administration, the leadership explicitly limits the pool of candidates to 'men' (andres), excluding women from this specific appointment and official role.

extracted_rules.resource: Equal access to daily food service
verbatim: [§184] Now in those days, when the number of the disciples was multiplying, there arose a grumbling of the Grecian Jews against the Hebrews because their widows were neglected in the daily service.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a systemic discrepancy in the distribution of resources based on the linguistic/cultural background (Grecian vs. Hebrew) of the recipients, where the minority group received less.

verbatim: [§208] He gave him the covenant of circumcision. So Abraham became the father of Isaac, and circumcised him the eighth day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a religious rule where infants are subjected to a specific physical procedure (circumcision) upon reaching the eighth day of life as part of a covenantal requirement.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§264] But Saul ravaged the assembly, entering into every house, and dragged both men and women off to prison.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic state or communal action where members of a specific religious group (the 'assembly' or Christians) are forcibly removed from their homes and imprisoned due to their beliefs.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§281] But Peter said to him, "May your silver perish with you, because you thought you could obtain the gift of God with money! [§282] You have neither part nor lot in this matter, for your heart isn't right before God."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a rule that spiritual authority (the laying on of hands) cannot be purchased and is restricted to those whose 'heart is right before God', effectively excluding those outside the faith or those attempting to use wealth to gain religious status.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§297] As they went on the way, they came to some water, and the eunuch said, "Behold, here is water. What is keeping me from being baptized?" [§298] He commanded the chariot to stand still, and they both went down into the water, both Philip and the eunuch, and he baptized him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates that ethnicity or status as a foreigner (the Ethiopian eunuch) does not bar an individual from the resource of religious baptism/initiation into the Christian community.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§273] But when they believed Philip preaching good news concerning the Kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions that Samaritans (a distinct ethnic/religious group) were granted access to the rite of baptism, showing the expansion of the group's boundaries.

verbatim: [§301] But Saul, still breathing threats and slaughter against the disciples of the Lord, went to the high priest, and asked for letters from him to the synagogues of Damascus, that if he found any who were of the Way, whether men or women, he might bring them bound to Jerusalem.
[§315] Here he has authority from the chief priests to bind all who call on your name.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a formal administrative process where the high priest issues 'letters' (warrants) granting an agent the power to arrest and transport members of a specific religious group ('the Way') across different jurisdictions (Damascus to Jerusalem).

verbatim: [§303] and asked for letters from him to the synagogues of Damascus, that if he found any who were of the Way, whether men or women, he might bring them bound to Jerusalem.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly notes that the authority to arrest and bind extends to women as well as men, clarifying that gender does not provide an exemption from these legal/religious proceedings.

verbatim: [§373] He said to them, "You yourselves know how it is an unlawful thing for a man who is a Jew to join himself or come to one of another nation, but God has shown me that I shouldn't call any man unholy or unclean."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that contemporary Jewish law or custom prohibited Jews from associating with or visiting people of other nations (Gentiles). Peter refers to this as a known 'unlawful thing' before explaining his new vision.

verbatim: [§392] "Can any man forbid the water, that these who have received the Holy Spirit as well as we should not be baptized?" [§393] He commanded them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a shift where Gentiles, previously excluded from the specific rites of the community (implied by the shock of the 'circumcision' group), are now granted the right to baptism based on divine approval.

verbatim: [§379] Peter opened his mouth and said, "Truly I perceive that God doesn't show favoritism; [§380] but in every nation he who fears him and works righteousness is acceptable to him."
extracted_rules.reasoning: Peter defines a new rule of practice where the 'resource' of being acceptable to God (and by extension the community) is extended to all nations, whereas it was previously restricted to the children of Israel.

verbatim: [§397] saying, 'You went in to uncircumcised men, and ate with them!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a contention based on traditional Jewish social boundaries where eating with uncircumcised individuals (Gentiles) was viewed as a violation of social/religious norms.

verbatim: [§413] They therefore who were scattered abroad by the oppression that arose about Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, speaking the word to no one except only to Jews.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage documents a specific practice of early preachers limiting their religious communication exclusively to Jews, thereby excluding Gentiles from the resource of their teaching.

verbatim: [§444] When Herod had sought for him, and didn't find him, he examined the guards, and commanded that they should be put to death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This describes a legal/disciplinary rule where the failure of guards to secure a prisoner resulted in their summary execution by the sovereign.

verbatim: [§445] ...they asked for peace, because their country depended on the king's country for food.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights the vulnerability of the people of Tyre and Sidon (foreigners to Herod's direct realm) who lacked independent food security and were dependent on the king's favor for survival.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§497] Paul and Barnabas spoke out boldly, and said, "It was necessary that God's word should be spoken to you first. Since indeed you thrust it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we turn to the Gentiles."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states a social-religious protocol where the 'word of God' was required to be presented to the Jews before other groups (Gentiles), establishing a rule of precedence based on religious/ethnic identity.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§490] and by him everyone who believes is justified from all things, from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies a limitation of the Mosaic law (accessible to Jews/proselytes) regarding justification, contrasting it with a new resource (justification through faith) now available to a broader group including Gentiles.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§468] The God of this people Israel chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they stayed as aliens in the land of Egypt , and with an uplifted arm, he led them out of it. [§469] For about the time of forty years he put up with them in the wilderness. [§470] When he had destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave them their land for an inheritance...
extracted_rules.reasoning: While describing historical events, the author identifies the group's status as 'aliens' (foreigners) as a state distinct from possessing land as an 'inheritance,' implying that as long as they were defined as foreigners in Egypt, they lacked the resource of land ownership.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in synagogue assemblies
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§494] Now when the synagogue broke up, many of the Jews and of the devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas; who, speaking to them, urged them to continue in the grace of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'proselytes' (converts/foreigners) as a distinct group from 'Jews' who nonetheless possess the right to participate in the synagogue assembly and religious instruction.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§501] But the Jews urged on the devout women of honorable estate, and the chief men of the city, and stirred up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and threw them out of their borders.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights a specific sub-group of women (those of 'honorable estate' or high status) who, alongside the 'chief men,' possessed the social power to incite official legal action (persecution and expulsion) from the city.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from circumcision and Mosaic Law
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§552] Therefore my judgment is that we don't trouble those from among the Gentiles who turn to God, [§553] but that we write to them that they abstain from the pollution of idols, from sexual immorality, from what is strangled, and from blood.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a formal council decision where Gentile converts are explicitly exempted from the 'yoke' of the full Mosaic law (circumcision), requiring only a limited set of prohibitions instead.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to walk and physical mobility
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§512] At Lystra a certain man sat, impotent in his feet, a cripple from his mother's womb, who never had walked.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies a specific group defined by health (congenital disability) as lacking the basic human resource of mobility/walking from birth.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§554] For Moses from generations of old has in every city those who preach him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a systemic rule of religious life where those of the Jewish faith/lineage have regular, institutional access to the reading and preaching of their law in every city.

extracted_rules.resource: Political and administrative authority in the assembly
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§527] When they had appointed elders for them in every assembly, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they had believed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the systematic installation of a specific group (elders) into positions of governance and oversight within the community assemblies.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from circumcision and Mosaic law
verbatim: [§561] For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit, and to us, to lay no greater burden on you than these necessary things: [§562] that you abstain from things sacrificed to idols, from blood, from things strangled, and from sexual immorality, from which if you keep yourselves, it will be well with you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly establishes a formal decree from the leadership in Jerusalem that non-Jewish converts (Gentiles) are not required to follow the full Mosaic law (circumcision), only a specific set of moral and dietary prohibitions.

verbatim: [§590] It happened, as we were going to prayer, that a certain girl having a spirit of divination met us, who brought her masters much gain by fortune telling.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes a slave girl whose labor (fortune-telling) generates profit specifically for her 'masters,' indicating that the income produced by a slave's activity belongs to the owner, not the individual performing the service.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from public beating without trial
verbatim: [§611] But Paul said to them, 'They have beaten us publicly, without a trial, men who are Romans, and have cast us into prison! Do they now release us secretly? No, most assuredly, but let them come themselves and bring us out!' [§612] The sergeants reported these words to the magistrates, and they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul invokes his status as a Roman citizen to highlight the illegality of his public beating and imprisonment without a trial. The fear of the magistrates confirms that Roman law provided specific protections against such treatment for citizens.

verbatim: [§611] But Paul said to them... 'No, most assuredly, but let them come themselves and bring us out!' [§612] The sergeants reported these words to the magistrates, and they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans, [§613] and they came and begged them. When they had brought them out, they asked them to depart from the city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul refuses a secret release, asserting that because he is a Roman citizen who was mistreated, the magistrates must personally and publicly escort him out. The magistrates' compliance shows an acknowledgment of the rights of a citizen to formal redress after a violation of due process.

verbatim: [§652] He found a certain Jew named Aquila, a man of Pontus by race, who had recently come from Italy, with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had commanded all the Jews to depart from Rome.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a historical-legal decree issued by the Emperor Claudius which explicitly removed the right of residence for the Jewish population in Rome, forcing their departure.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to prosecute internal religious matters
extracted_rules.rule: Roman refusal of Jewish religious litigation
verbatim: [§664] But when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, "If indeed it were a matter of wrong or of wicked crime, Jews, it would be reasonable that I should bear with you; [§665] but if they are questions about words and names and your own law, look to it yourselves. For I don't want to be a judge of these matters."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Roman proconsul Gallio establishes a jurisdictional rule: the Roman state provides legal resources for crimes and civil wrongs, but denies those resources to Jews seeking to adjudicate matters of their own religious law.

verbatim: [§633] Others said, "He seems to be advocating foreign demons," because he preached Jesus and the resurrection. [§634] They took hold of him, and brought him to the Areopagus, saying, "May we know what this new teaching is, which is spoken by you?"
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Athens, Paul (a newcomer) is detained and brought before a governing council (the Areopagus) to account for preaching 'foreign' deities, indicating that non-citizens did not have the unrestricted right to introduce new religious doctrines without official inquiry.

verbatim: [§717] If therefore Demetrius and the craftsmen who are with him have a matter against anyone, the courts are open, and there are proconsuls. Let them press charges against one another.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The town clerk acknowledges that craftsmen as a professional group have the legal standing to access the court system and proconsuls to settle specific grievances rather than resorting to riots.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to settle matters in assembly
verbatim: [§718] But if you seek anything about other matters, it will be settled in the regular assembly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies that for general matters outside of private litigation, the 'regular assembly' (the ekklesia of citizens) is the proper legal venue for resolution.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be heard in public
verbatim: [§712] They brought Alexander out of the multitude, the Jews putting him forward. Alexander beckoned with his hand, and would have made a defense to the people. [§713] But when they perceived that he was a Jew, all with one voice for a time of about two hours cried out, 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Ephesian multitude actively prevents a speaker from making a defense solely based on the identification of his religious/ethnic group (Jew), shouting him down for two hours.

verbatim: [§716] For you have brought these men here, who are neither robbers of temples nor blasphemers of your goddess.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The town clerk's defense of the accused rests on the legal standard that they have not committed the crimes of temple robbery or blasphemy, implying these are social/legal protections afforded to the established religious institutions.

verbatim: [§738] From Miletus he sent to Ephesus, and called to himself the elders of the assembly. [...] [§749] Take heed, therefore, to yourselves, and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to shepherd the assembly of the Lord and God which he purchased with his own blood.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific class of people (elders) and assigns them the specific role and resource of presiding over/shepherding the community (the flock) as 'overseers'.

verbatim: [§753] Now, brothers, I entrust you to God, and to the word of his grace, which is able to build up, and to give you the inheritance among all those who are sanctified.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a group based on their religious state ('those who are sanctified') and identifies the 'inheritance' as a resource they are given access to.

verbatim: [§754] I coveted no one's silver, or gold, or clothing. [§755] You yourselves know that these hands ministered to my necessities, and to those who were with me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul establishes a norm for his group (itinerant teachers/preachers) stating that they should provide for their own material needs (necessities) through manual labor rather than taking resources from others.

verbatim: [§788] crying out, 'Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches all men everywhere against the people, and the law, and this place. Moreover, he also brought Greeks into the temple, and has defiled this holy place!'
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an uproar based on the accusation that a non-Jew (Greek) was brought into the Temple, which is stated to have 'defiled' the holy place, establishing a clear rule of exclusion for that group from that specific location.

verbatim: [§785] But concerning the Gentiles who believe, we have written our decision that they should observe no such thing, except that they should keep themselves from food offered to idols, from blood, from strangled things, and from sexual immorality.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The elders explicitly state that while Jews are expected to observe the Law of Moses, the group defined as Gentiles are granted a formal exemption from the full scope of those customs, requiring only a specific subset of prohibitions.

extracted_rules.resource: Permission to speak to the public
verbatim: [§799] But Paul said, 'I am a Jew, from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no insignificant city. I beg you, allow me to speak to the people.' [§800] When he had given him permission, Paul, standing on the stairs, beckoned with his hand to the people.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul invokes his status as a citizen of a specific city to request and successfully receive permission from the Roman commander to address the crowd, linking legal status/origin to the right of public speech.

verbatim: [§826] When they had tied him up with thongs, Paul asked the centurion who stood by, "Is it lawful for you to scourge a man who is a Roman, and not found guilty?" [§827] When the centurion heard it, he went to the commanding officer and told him, "Watch what you are about to do, for this man is a Roman!"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a legal boundary where Roman citizens are protected from scourging (corporal punishment) without a formal conviction. The immediate reaction of the officers confirms this is a binding rule in their society.

verbatim: [§830] Immediately those who were about to examine him departed from him, and the commanding officer also was afraid when he realized that he was a Roman, because he had bound him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The commanding officer's fear stems from the realization that even binding a Roman citizen for the purpose of examination (torture) was a violation of their rights and a punishable offense for the officer.

extracted_rules.rule: High priest immunity from verbal abuse
verbatim: [§836] Those who stood by said, "Do you malign God's high priest?" [§837] Paul said, "I didn't know, brothers, that he was high priest. For it is written, 'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage cites a specific legal/religious rule ('it is written') that forbids speaking evil of a ruler or high priest, granting them a specific immaterial resource of protected status from verbal disparagement.

verbatim: [§805] I persecuted this Way to the death, binding and delivering into prisons both men and women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul describes his historical actions under the authority of the high priest. The explicit mention of 'women' alongside men shows that within the religious persecution of that society, gender did not provide immunity from being bound or imprisoned.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§859] "This man was seized by the Jews, and was about to be killed by them, when I came with the soldiers and rescued him, having learned that he was a Roman."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The commander Claudius Lysias explicitly justifies his military intervention and the rescue of the prisoner from a local mob/council based on the fact that the individual was a Roman citizen.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§895] To whom I answered that it is not the custom of the Romans to give up any man to destruction, before the accused have met the accusers face to face, and have had opportunity to make his defense concerning the matter laid against him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Festus, the Roman governor, states that Roman legal custom (lex) forbids condemning or handing over a person until they have been granted a defense in the presence of their accusers.

extracted_rules.resource: Right of appeal to the Emperor
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§906] but if none of those things is true that these accuse me of, no one can give me up to them. I appeal to Caesar! [§907] Then Festus, when he had conferred with the council, answered, "You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go."
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates the 'provocatio', the right of a Roman citizen to have their case transferred from a provincial governor to the Emperor's jurisdiction in Rome.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to trial by Roman courts
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§905] But Paul said, "I am standing before Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be tried."
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul asserts that as a Roman, he should be tried in a Roman court rather than being handed over to a religious or local council (the Sanhedrin).

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§890] He ordered the centurion that Paul should be kept in custody, and should have some privileges, and not to forbid any of his friends to serve him or to visit him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Roman governor Felix grants a specific legal allowance for a prisoner in 'custodia libera' to receive material support and company from associates.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§895] For it seems to me unreasonable, in sending a prisoner, not to also specify the charges against him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Festus expresses a procedural norm that Roman law requires specific legal charges to be articulated when a prisoner is forwarded for higher judgment.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to confront accusers and make defense
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§911] To whom I answered that it is not the custom of the Romans to give up any man to destruction, before the accused have met the accusers face to face, and have had opportunity to make his defense concerning the matter laid against him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Roman governor Festus explicitly describes a legal custom/rule of the Roman legal system that guarantees the right to a face-to-face defense against accusers before a final sentence is passed.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to appeal for imperial judgment
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§916] But when Paul had appealed to be kept for the decision of the emperor, I commanded him to be kept until I could send him to Caesar. [...] [§955] Agrippa said to Festus, 'This man might have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paul, as a Roman citizen (established in context of Acts), exercises the right of 'provocatio' to the Emperor. This is shown as a binding rule that even a governor must follow, effectively removing the case from the local jurisdiction.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from detention without formal charges
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§922] For it seems to me unreasonable, in sending a prisoner, not to also specify the charges against him. [...] [§954] When they had withdrawn, they spoke one to another, saying, 'This man does nothing worthy of death or of bonds.'
extracted_rules.reasoning: Festus identifies a legal/procedural norm where holding or transferring a prisoner without specific criminal charges is considered 'unreasonable,' implying a rule requiring factual legal justification for the loss of liberty.

verbatim: [§998] The soldiers' counsel was to kill the prisoners, so that none of them would swim out and escape. [§999] But the centurion, desiring to save Paul, stopped them from their purpose
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a standard military counsel in Roman context where guards were liable for prisoners; the proposed action to kill all prisoners to prevent escape highlights their lack of a right to life in emergency situations, even though the centurion intervened in this specific instance.

verbatim: [§956] ...they delivered Paul and certain other prisoners to a centurion named Julius, of the Augustan band. ... Julius treated Paul kindly, and gave him permission to go to his friends and refresh himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes that prisoners are legally delivered into the custody of military officers (centurions), who hold total control over their movement and must grant specific permission for even basic social contact.

verbatim: [§986] As the sailors were trying to flee out of the ship, and had lowered the boat into the sea... [§987] Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, 'Unless these stay in the ship, you can't be saved.' [§988] Then the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In this military-led transport, the soldiers exercise physical force to prevent the civilian sailors from abandoning the vessel, effectively coercing their labor and presence.

verbatim: [§1020] But when the Jews spoke against it, I was constrained to appeal to Caesar, not that I had anything about which to accuse my nation.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Paul, who is established in previous chapters as a Roman citizen, exercising the legal right to have his case heard by the Emperor (Caesar) when the local proceedings are contested.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to private lodging during trial
verbatim: [§1017] When we entered into Rome, the centurion delivered the prisoners to the captain of the guard, but Paul was allowed to stay by himself with the soldier who guarded him.
[§1031] Paul stayed two whole years in his own rented house, and received all who went in to him
extracted_rules.reasoning: While other prisoners are delivered to the captain of the guard (implying standard prison), Paul is granted the privilege of 'custodia libera' or private lodging under guard, a common rule for citizens of status awaiting trial.

extracted_rules.rule: Protection from capital punishment without cause
verbatim: [§1019] who, when they had examined me, desired to set me free, because there was no cause of death in me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Roman examination concluded that without a specific legal cause or crime committed, the prisoner had a right to be set free rather than executed, reflecting Roman legal standards regarding the capital punishment of subjects/citizens.
